text: Colovestical fistulas can arise from injury to the colon or bladder, from diverticular abscess, or from cancer. The inflammation that causes the fistula also creates a hostile operative field within the abdomen. As both the colon and the bladder have a defect present, the dissection plane will favor the bladder. Once the bladder is separated from the colon, the defect will be closed in two layers. Some surgeons advocate not closing this defect. However, I feel it should be closed if possible. The inner layer is closed with an absorbable suture in a running fashion. As the bladder is inflamed and thickened, this layer may not completely re-approximate the defect. The outer layer is closed using a longer lasting absorbable suture that will imbricate the adventitial layer and the pelvic peritoneum. To allow for appropriate healing of the bladder repair, the patient will require a decompressive Foley catheter for seven to fourteen days after the operation.

After completing the bladder repair, the surgeon proceeds with a robotic sigmoid colectomy. The left ureter is identified in the retroperitoneal space, with visible vermiculation, ensuring its protection before safely mobilizing the colon.

The surgeon identifies the rectosigmoid junction by the coalescence of the colonic tinea. A tunnel is created in the mesocolon, and the distal colon is transected. The proximal colon is transected to ensure sufficient length for an anastomosis. Using the fluorescence lens of the da Vinci robot and injectable green dye, the blood supply at the colon's free edges is visualized. The proximal edge of the staple line is removed, and an anvil is placed into the colonic lumen with a purse-string suture for end-to-end anastomosis. The surgical assistant inserts the EEA stapler through the rectum, releasing the spike through the distal staple line. The anvil is joined to the EEA stapler, creating the anastomosis. A leak test is performed by occluding the proximal colon, filling the pelvis with saline, and insufflating air through the rectum to ensure an airtight seal. All sutures and the colonic specimen are removed, completing the operation.
